Source-Based Description & Context: The Fight for Sovereignty: This was the definitive assertion of the Métis as a "New Nation." Triggered by the Pemmican Proclamation—which sought to monopolize the Métis' primary trade and food source—this skirmish saw 60 Métis defeat a larger HBC force in minutes. It proved the Métis were a political and military power in the West, not just "employees" or "squatters."
Historical Figures & Connections: Cuthbert Grant; Pierre Falcon; Governor Semple (HBC).

Source-Based Description & Context: The Fight for the Hunt: A massive defense of the St. François Xavier hunting brigade against roughly 2,000 Dakota warriors. Beyond a territorial dispute, this was about the survival of the bison hunt—the engine of the Métis economy. The use of a "moving fortress" (carts in a circle with rifle pits dug beneath) became the blueprint for Métis defensive warfare.
Historical Figures & Connections: Jean Baptiste Falcon; Pascal Breland; Father Louis Laflèche.

Source-Based Description & Context: The Spark of Resistance: Sparked by the Canadian government’s refusal to grant land titles to Métis settlers along the South Saskatchewan River. When a parley between the NWMP and Métis went wrong, a 30-minute firefight erupted. This victory forced the Canadian government to acknowledge the Métis' military resolve, though it led to the immediate dispatch of 5,000 troops.
Historical Figures & Connections: Gabriel Dumont; Isidore Dumont; Supt. Leif Crozier.

Source-Based Description & Context: The Guerilla Defense: Fought on the homestead of the Tourond family, this was an ambush designed to stall the Canadian army's march on Batoche. Dumont utilized the steep, brush-heavy coulee to trap Middleton’s troops. The battle proved that the Métis' superior knowledge of the local terrain could neutralize the army's superior numbers and technology (like the Gatling gun).
Historical Figures & Connections: Gabriel Dumont; General Middleton; The Tourond Family.

Source-Based Description & Context: The Final Stand: This was an all-out defense of the community’s headquarters and homes. For four days, Métis families (men, women, and children) lived in pits while under heavy artillery fire. They weren't just fighting for Riel; they were fighting to prevent the displacement of their entire society. The resistance ended only when ammunition was replaced by stones and nails in their rifles.
Historical Figures & Connections: Louis Riel; Gabriel Dumont; Josèphte Tourond; Marguerite Riel.

Source-Based Description & Context: The Retreat to the North: The final military engagement on Canadian soil. After the fall of Batoche, Métis and First Nations families retreated into the muskeg and bush to avoid capture. This battle was a desperate rearguard action to protect the elders and children from the pursuing North-West Mounted Police.
